1
Sarah is in her car.  She is on her way to work.
She’s driving to work. (= She is driving …)
This means: she is driving now, at the time of speaking.
The action is not finished.

units.search_text:

Present simple (I do)
Unit
2
We use the present simple to talk about things in general.  We use it to say that something happens all the 
time or repeatedly, or that something is true in general:
Nurses look aft er patients in hospitals. 
I usually go away at weekends.

units.search_text:
A
Present continuous and present simple 2
(I am doing and I do)
Unit
4
We use continuous forms (I’m waiting, it’s raining etc.) for actions and happenings that have started but 
not finished.  
Some verbs (for example, know and like) are not normally used in this way.  We don’t say ‘I am knowing’,

5
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart was an Austrian

and wrote more than 600 pieces of music. 
He was only 35 years old when he died. 
lived/started/wrote/was/died are all past simple

No, I haven’t been to India.

units.search_text:

13

A
Study this example situation:
Ten minutes later:
The present perfect (something has happened) is a present tense.  It tells us about the situation now. 
‘Tom has lost his key’ = he doesn’t have his key now (see Unit 7).

units.title: Present perfect and past 2 (I have done and I did)
units.search_text:
Unit
14
Present perfect and past 2 (I have done and I did)
A
We do not use the present perfect (I have done) when we talk about a finished time (for example,
yesterday / last year / ten minutes ago etc.).  We use a past tense:
It was very cold yesterday. (not has been) 
Paul and Lucy arrived ten min

The past perfect (simple) is had + past participle (gone/seen/finished etc.).
Sometimes we talk about something that happened in the past:
 Sarah arrived

units.title: Have and have got
units.search_text:
Unit
17
have and have got
A
have and have got (= for possession, relationships, illnesses, appointments etc.)
You can use have or have got.  There is no difference in meaning.  You can say: 
They have a new car.    or    They’ve got a new car. 
Lisa has two brothers.    or    Lisa has got two brothe

used to (do)

18
a few years ago
these days
Nicola doesn’t travel much these days.
She prefers to stay at home. 
But she used to travel a lot. 
She used to go away two or three times a year. 
She used to travel a lot = she travelled oft en in the past, but 
she do

units.title: Present tenses (I am doing / I do) for the future
units.search_text:
Unit
19
Present tenses (I am doing / I do) for the future
A
Present continuous (I am doing) with a future meaning
I’m doing something (tomorrow etc.) = I have already decided and arranged to do it:
a: What are you doing on Saturday evening? (not What do you do)
b: I’m going to the cinema. (not I go)

units.title: (I'm) going to (do)
units.search_text:
I’m going to (do)
Unit
20
A
I am going to do something = I have already decided to do it, I intend to do it:
‘Are you going to eat anything?’ 
‘No, I’m not hungry.’
a: I hear Sarah won the lottery.  What is she going to do with the money? 
b: She’s going to buy a new car. 
I’m just going to make a q

units.title: Will and shall 1
units.search_text:
A
will and shall 1
We use I’ll … (= I will) when we’ve just decided to do something.  When we say ‘I’ll do something’, 
we announce our decision:
Oh, I left  the door open.  I’ll go and shut it.
‘What would you like to drink?’ 
‘I’ll have orange juice, please.’
‘Did you call Max?’ 
‘Oh no, I forgot.

units.title: Will and shall 2
units.search_text:
A
Some more examples:
They’ve been away a long time.  When they return, they’ll find a lot of changes here.
‘Where will you be this time next year?’ 
‘I’ll be in Japan.’
That plate is hot. If you touch it, you’ll burn yourself.
Anna looks completely diff erent now.  You won’t recognise her.
When wil

now

units.search_text:
A
Some more examples:
We’ll go out when it stops raining. (not when it will stop)
When you are here again, you must come and see us. (not When you will be)
Don’t forget to lock the door when you go out. (not will go)
The same thing happens aft er while / before / aft er / as soon as / until:

units.title: Can, could and (be) able to
units.search_text:
can, could and (be) able to
We use can to say that something is possible or allowed, or that somebody has the ability to do 
something.  We use can + infinitive (can do / can see etc.):
We can see the lake from our hotel.
‘I don’t have a pen.’ 
‘You can use mine.’
Can you speak any foreign languages

units.title: Could (do) and could have (done)
units.search_text:
Unit
27
could (do) and could have (done)
A
Sometimes could is the past of can (see Unit 26):
Listen.  I can hear something. (now)
I listened.  I could hear something. (past)
But could is not always past.  We also use could for possible actions now or in the future, especially to 
make suggestions.

units.title: Must and can't
units.search_text:
must and can’t
Unit
28
A
Study this example:
We use must to say that we believe something is certain:
You’ve been travelling all day.  You must be tired.
(travelling is tiring and you’ve been travelling all day, so you must be tired)
‘Joe is a hard worker.’ 
‘Joe?  You must be joking.  He doesn’t do

units.title: May and might 1
units.search_text:
Unit
29
may and might 1
A
Study this example situation:
We use may or might to say that something is possible.  You can use may or might:
It may be true.    or    It might be true. (= perhaps it is true)
She might know.    or    She may know.
The negative forms are may not and might not:
It may not

units.title: May and might 2
units.search_text:
may and might 2
Unit
30
A
We use may and might to talk about possible actions or happenings in the future:
I haven’t decided where to go on holiday.  I may go to Ireland. (= perhaps I will go there)
Take an umbrella with you.  It might rain later. (= perhaps it will rain)
The bus isn’t always on tim

units.title: Have to and must
units.search_text:
A
have to and must
I have to do something = it is necessary to do it, I am obliged to do it:
You can’t turn right here.  You have to turn left .
I have to wear glasses for reading.
Robert can’t come out with us this evening.
 
He has to work late.
Last week Tina broke her arm and had to go to hospit

must     mustn’t     needn’t

32

A
must and mustn’t
You must do something = it is necessary that you do it:
Don’t tell anybody what I said.  You must

units.title: Should 1
units.search_text:
should 1
Unit
33
A
You should do something = it is a good thing to do or the right thing to do.
You can use should to give advice or to give an opinion:
You look tired.  You should go to bed.
The government should do more to improve
schools.
a: Should we invite Stephanie to the party?
b: Yes, I thin

units.title: Should 2
units.search_text:
Unit
34
should 2
A
You can use should after:
insist        demand        recommend        suggest        propose
I insisted that he should apologise.
Doctors recommend that everyone should eat plenty of fruit.
What do you suggest we should do?
Many people are demanding that something should be done

units.title: Had better / It's time ...
units.search_text:
should 1 ➜ Unit 33
I’d better …     it’s time …
Unit
35
A
had better (I’d better / you’d better etc.)
I’d better do something = it is advisable to do it.  If I don’t do it, there will be a problem or a danger:
I have to meet Amy in ten minutes.  I’d better go now or I’ll be late.
‘Shall I take an um

units.title: Would
units.search_text:
Unit
36
would
A
We use would (’d) / wouldn’t when we imagine a situation or action (= we think of something that is
not real):
It would be nice to buy a new car, but we can’t aff ord it.
I’d love to live by the sea.
a: Shall I tell Chris what happened?
b: No, I wouldn’t say anything.
(= I wouldn’t s

 Can you wait a moment, please?

units.title: Wish
units.search_text:
wish
We say ‘wish somebody luck / all the best / success’ etc. :
I wish you all the best in the future.
I saw Mark before the exam and he wished me luck. 
We say ‘wish somebody something’ (luck, happiness etc.), but we do not say ‘I wish something happens’.  
We use hope in this situation:
I’m sorry

units.search_text:
Passive 1 (is done / was done)
Unit
42
A
Study this example:
When we use an active verb, we say what the subject does:
My grandfather was a builder.  He built this house in 1981.
It’s a big company.  It employs two hundred people.
When we use a passive verb, we say what happens to the subject:
‘How

Passive 3

44
I was off ered … / we were given … etc.
Some verbs can have two objects.  For example, give:
My grandfather gave  me  this watch.
It is possible to make two passive sentences:
I was given this watch (by my grandfather).   or
This watch was given to me (by my grandfather).

45

It is said that he is 108 years old.
or  He is said to be 108 years old.

units.search_text:
have something done

46
If you have something done, you arrange for somebody to do it for you.
Compare:
Lisa repaired the roof. (= she repaired it herself)
 
Lisa had the roof repaired. (= she arranged for somebody else to repair it)

You saw Paul yesterday and you want to tell somebody

You can repeat Paul’s words (direct speech):
Paul said, ‘I’m feeling ill.’
Or you can use reported speech:
Paul said that he

Reported speech 2

48
We do not always change the verb in reported speech.  If the situation is still the same, it is not necessary to 
change the verb to the past.  For example:
direct 
Paul said, ‘My new job is boring.’
 
reported Paul said that his new job is boring.

units.title: Verb + -ing or to ... 3 (like, would like etc.)
units.search_text:

Unit
58
A
like / love / hate
When you talk about repeated actions, you can use -ing or to … after these verbs.
So you can say:
Do you like getting up early?    or    Do you like to get up early?
Stephanie hates flying.    or    Stephanie hates to fly.

prefer and would rather

prefer to … and prefer -ing
When you say what you prefer in general, you can use prefer to … or prefer -ing:

You can say:

to something else

A
to … (afraid to do) and 
preposition + -ing (afraid of -ing)
afraid to (do) and afraid of (do)ing
Unit
66
I am afraid to do something = 
I don’t want to do it because it is dangerous or the result could be bad.
This part of town is dangerous.  People are afraid to walk here at night.
(= they don’t

units.title: See somebody do and see somebody doing
units.search_text:
Past simple (I did) ➜ Unit 5
Past continuous (I was doing) ➜ Unit 6
A
see somebody do and see somebody doing
Study this example situation:
Unit
67
Tom got into his car and drove off .  You saw this.
You can say:
I saw Tom get into his car and drive off .
We say ‘I saw him do something’ (= he did it

units.title: -ing clauses (Feeling tired, I went to bed early.)
units.search_text:
A
-ing clauses (He hurt his knee playing football.)
Study this example:
Unit
68
Kate is in the kitchen.  She’s making coff ee.
You can say:
Kate is in the kitchen making coff ee.
You can use -ing in this way when two things happen 
at the same time:
A man ran out of the house shouting.
(= he ran out

units.title: Countable and uncountable 1
units.search_text:
A
Countable and uncountable 1
A noun can be countable or uncountable:
Unit
69
Countable
I eat a banana every day.
I like bananas.
Banana is a countable noun.
A countable noun can be singular (banana)
or plural (bananas).
We can use numbers with countable nouns.
So we can say one banana, two bananas

units.title: Countable and uncountable 2
units.search_text:
Countable and uncountable 1 ➜ Unit 69
American English ➜ Appendix 7
A
Countable and uncountable 2
Many nouns are sometimes countable, and sometimes uncountable.  Usually there is a difference in meaning. 
Compare:
Coffee/tea/juice/beer etc. (drinks) are normally uncountable:
 I don’t like coffee ver

units.title: Countable nouns with a/an and some
units.search_text:
A
Countable nouns with a/an and some
Countable nouns can be singular or plural: 
a dog 
a child 
the evening 
this party 
an umbrella
dogs 
some children 
the evenings 
these parties 
two umbrellas
Before singular countable nouns you can use a/an:
Bye!  Have a nice evening. 
Do you need an umbrella?

units.title: A/an and the
units.search_text:
a/an ➜ Unit 71
the ➜ Units 73–78
A
Study this example:
Joe says ‘a sandwich’, ‘an apple’ because this is 
the first time he talks about them.
Joe now says ‘the sandwich’, ‘the apple’
because we know which sandwich and 
which apple he means – the sandwich and
the apple he had for lunch.
I had a sandw

units.title: The 1
units.search_text:
a/an ➜ Unit 71
a/an and the ➜ Unit 72
the 2–4 ➜ Units 74–76
Names with and without the ➜ Units 77–78
A
the 1
We use the when there is only one of something:
Have you ever crossed the equator?
(there is only one equator)
Our apartment is on the tenth floor.
Buenos Aires is the capital of Argentina.

units.title: The 2 (school / the school etc.)
units.search_text:
Compare school and the school:
the 2   (school / the school etc.)
Unit
74
Ellie is ten years old.  Every day she goes to 
school.  She’s at school now.  School starts  
at 9 and finishes at 3.
We say a child goes to school or is at school 
(as a student).  We are not thinking of a specific 
school.

units.title: The 3 (children / the children)
units.search_text:
the 1–2 ➜ Units 73–74
the + adjective (the young / the English etc.) ➜ Unit 76
When we are talking about things or people in general, we do not use the:
I’m afraid of dogs. (not the dogs)
(dogs = dogs in general, not a specific group of dogs)
Doctors are usually paid more than teachers.
Do you know

units.title: The 4 (the giraffe / the telephone / the old etc.)
units.search_text:
a/an and the ➜ Unit 72
the ➜ Units 73–75
Names with and without the ➜ Units 77–78
Study these sentences:
The giraff e is the tallest of all animals.
The bicycle is an excellent means of transport.
When was the camera invented?
The dollar is the currency of the United States.
In these examples, the …

units.title: Names with and without the 1
units.search_text:
Names with and without the 2 ➜ Unit 78
We do not use the with names of people (‘Helen’, ‘Helen Taylor’ etc.).  In the same way, we do
not use the with most names of places.  For example:
continents
countries, states etc.
islands
cities, towns etc.
mountains
Africa (not the Africa), South America
Fra

units.title: Names with and without the 2
units.search_text:
Names with and without the 1 ➜ Unit 77
A
Names without the
We do not use the with names of most city streets/roads/squares/parks etc. :
Union Street (not the …) 
Fifth Avenue 
Hyde Park
Abbey Road 
Broadway 
Times Square
Names of many public buildings and institutions (airports, stations, universiti

units.title: Singular and plural
units.search_text:
American English ➜ Appendix 7
A
Sometimes we use a plural noun for one thing that has two parts.  For example:
trousers (two legs)
also jeans/tights/shorts/pants
pyjamas
(top and bottom)
glasses
binoculars
scissors
These words are plural, so they take a plural verb:
My trousers are too long. (not My

units.title: Noun + noun (a bus driver / a headache)
units.search_text:
-’s and of … ➜ Unit 81
a week’s holiday / three weeks’ holiday etc. ➜ Unit 81E
You can use two nouns together (noun + noun) to mean one thing/person/idea etc. :
a bus driver 
 
income tax 
the city centre 
 
an apple tree
The first noun is like an adjective.  It tells us what kind of thing/person/id

units.title: 's (the girl's name) and of (the name of the book)
units.search_text:
-’s (your sister’s name) and  
of … (the name of the book)
We use -’s (apostrophe + s) mostly for people or animals:
Tom’s computer isn’t working. (not the computer of Tom)
How old are Chris’s children?   (not the children of Chris)
What’s (= What is) your sister’s name?
What’s Tom’s sister’s name?

units.title: Myself/yourself/themselves etc.
units.search_text:
get dressed / get married etc. ➜ Unit 44D
by myself / by yourself etc. ➜ Unit 83D
myself/yourself/themselves etc.
Study this example:
The reflexive pronouns are:
singular (-self) 
myself 
yourself (one person) 
himself/herself/itself
plural (-selves) 
ourselves 
yourselves (more than one) 
themselve

units.title: A friend of mine / my own house / on my own
units.search_text:
myself/yourself/themselves etc. ➜ Unit 82
a friend of mine  my own house
on my own / by myself
a friend of mine / a friend of yours etc.
We say ‘(a friend) of mine/yours/his/hers/ours/theirs’.  
A friend of mine = one of my friends:
I’m going to a wedding on Saturday.  A friend of mine is getting ma

units.title: There ... and it ...
units.search_text:
there … and it …
Study this example:
We use there … when we talk about something for the first time, to say that it exists:
There’s a new restaurant in Hill Street.
I’m sorry I’m late.  There was a lot of traff ic. (not It was a lot of traff ic)
Things are very expensive now.  There has been a big r

units.title: Some and any
units.search_text:
not … any ➜ Unit 86
some of / any of … ➜ Unit 88
hardly any ➜ Unit 101C
some and any
In general we use some (also somebody/someone/something) in positive sentences and any 
(also anybody etc.) in negative sentences:
We use any in the following sentences because the meaning is negative:
She went out

units.title: No/none/any / Nothing/nobody etc.
units.search_text:
no/none/any 
 
nothing/nobody etc.
no and none
We use no + noun (no bus, no shops etc.).   
no = not a or not any:
We had to walk home.  There was no bus. (= There wasn’t a bus.)
Sarah will have no trouble finding a job. (= Sarah won’t have any trouble …)
There were no shops open. (= There weren’t a

units.title: Much, many, little, few, a lot, plenty
units.search_text:
Countable and uncountable ➜ Units 69–70
much, many, little, few, a lot, plenty
We use much and little with uncountable nouns: 
much luck 
 
much time 
 
little energy 
 
little money
We use many and few with plural nouns:
many friends 
 
many people 
 
few cars 
 
few children
We use a lot of / lots

units.title: All / all of / most / most of / no / none of etc.
units.search_text:
all / all of  most / most of  no / none of etc.
all 
 
some 
 
any 
 
most 
 
much 
 
many 
 
(a) littl e 
(a) few 
 
no
You can use these words with a noun (some food / few books etc.):
All cars have wheels.
Some cars can go faster than others.
Many people drive too fast.
I go away most weekends.
I

units.title: Both / both of / neither / neither of / either / either of
units.search_text:
neither do I / I don’t either ➜ Unit 51C
any ➜ Units 85–86
none ➜ Units 86A, 88
all ➜ Unit 88
both of whom / neither of which ➜ Unit 96B
both ➜ Unit 110D
both / both of  neither / neither of       
either / either of
We use both/neither/either for two things.  
You can use these words with a noun (b

units.title: All, every and whole
units.search_text:
all   every   whole 
everybody/everyone/everything and all 
We say:
Everybody was happy.     or     Everyone was happy. (not all were happy)
He thinks he knows everything. (not knows all)
Our holiday was a disaster.  Everything went wrong. (not all went wrong)
We do not often use all alone in this w

units.title: Each and every
units.search_text:
A
each and every
Unit
91
each other ➜ Unit 82C
all and every ➜ Unit 90
Each and every are similar.  Oft en it is possible to use each or every:
Each time I see you, you look diff erent.    or    Every time I see you …
But each and every are not exactly the same.
We use each when we think of things
s

units.search_text:
Relative clauses 1: clauses with who/that/which
Unit
92
A
Study this example situation:
Last week we had a party and a lot of people came.  Everybody enjoyed it.
 
Everybody  who came to the party  enjoyed it.
A clause is a part of a sentence.  A relative clause tells us which person or thing (or wh

93
Relative clauses 2: 
clauses with and without who/that/which

The woman  who  lives next door to me is a doctor. (or The woman that lives …)
The woman  lives next  door to me 
who (= the woman) is the subject
Where are the keys  that  were on th

units.title: Relative clauses 3: whose/whom/where
units.search_text:
Relative clauses 3: whose/whom/where
Unit
94
A
whose
Study this example situation:
When we were driving home, we saw some people standing by the 
road.  Their car had broken down, so we stopped to help them.
We helped some people whose car had broken down. 
(= their car had broken down)
We use whose

units.title: Relative clauses 4: extra information clauses (1)
units.search_text:
Relative clauses 4: extra information clauses (1)
Unit
95
A
There are two types of relative clause.  In these examples, the relative clauses are underlined.

Type 1
The woman who lives next door to me  
is a doctor.
Grace works for a company that makes 
furniture.
We stayed at the hotel (tha

units.title: Relative clauses 5: extra information clauses (2)
units.search_text:
Relative clauses 5: extra information clauses (2)
Unit
96
A
preposition + whom/which
You can use a preposition + whom (for people) and which (for things).  
So you can say:
to whom 
 
with whom 
about whom 
etc.
of which 
without which 
from which 
etc.
Mr Lee, to whom I spoke at the meeting, is int

units.title: -ing and -ed clauses (the woman talking to Tom, the boy injured in the accident)
units.search_text:
-ing and -ed clauses (the woman talking to Tom,
the boy injured in the accident)
Unit
97
A
A clause is a part of a sentence.  Some clauses begin with -ing.  For example:
Who is the woman talking to Tom? 
The boy injured in the accident was taken to hospital.
-ing clause
TOM
the woman 
talking to Tom

units.title: Adjectives ending in -ing and -ed (boring/bored etc.)
units.search_text:
Adjectives ending in -ing and -ed  (boring/bored etc.)
Unit
98
A
Many adjectives end in -ing and -ed, for example: boring and bored.
Study this example situation:
Jane has been doing the same job for 
a very long time.  Every day she does 
exactly the same thing again and again.  
She doesn’t enjoy

units.title: Adjectives: a nice new house, you look tired
units.search_text:
Adjectives: a nice new house, you look tired
Unit
99
A
Sometimes we use two or more adjectives together:
My brother lives in a nice new house.
In the kitchen there was a beautiful large round wooden table.
Adjectives like new/large/round/wooden are fact adjectives.  They give us information about 
a

units.title: Adjectives and adverbs 1 (quick/quickly)
units.search_text:
Adjectives and adverbs 1 (quick/quickly)
Unit
100
A
You can say:
Our holiday was too short – the time passed very quickly.
Two people were seriously injured in the accident.
Quickly and seriously are adverbs.  Many adverbs are adjective + -ly:
adjective 
quick 
serious 
careful 
bad 
heavy 
terrible

units.title: Adjectives and adverbs 2 (well, fast, late, hard/hardly)
units.search_text:
A
Adjectives and adverbs 2
(well, fast, late, hard/hardly)
good and well
Good is an adjective.  The adverb is well:
Your English is good. 
but    You speak English well.
Sophie is a good pianist.    but    Sophie plays the piano well.
We use well (not good) with past participles (known/educated etc.

units.title: So and such
units.search_text:
not so … as ➜ Unit 107A
such as  ➜ Unit 117A
A
so and such
Compare so and such:
Unit
102
We use so + adjective/adverb:
so stupid 
so quick
so nice  
so quickly
I didn’t like the book.  The story was so
 
stupid.
Everything happened so quickly.
We use such + noun:
such a story
such people
We also use

units.title: Enough and too
units.search_text:
to … and for … (purpose) ➜ Unit 64      Adjective + to … (diff icult to understand etc.) ➜ Unit 65
A
enough and too
enough
Enough goes aft er adjectives and adverbs:
I can’t run very far.  I’m not fit enough. (not enough fit)
Let’s go.  We’ve waited long enough.
Enough normally goes before nouns:
We

units.title: Quite, pretty, rather and fairly
units.search_text:
A
quite, pretty, rather and fairly
Quite and pretty are similar in meaning (= less than ‘very’, but more than ‘a little’):
I’m surprised you haven’t heard of her.  She’s quite famous.  or   She’s pretty famous.  
(= less than ‘very famous’, but more than ‘a little famous’)
Anna lives quite near me,

units.title: Comparison 1 (cheaper, more expensive etc.)
units.search_text:
Comparative 2–3 ➜ Units 106–107
Superlative (cheapest / most expensive etc.) ➜ Unit 108
A
Comparative 1 (cheaper, more expensive etc.)
Look at these examples:
Unit
105
How shall we travel?  Shall we drive or go by train?
Let’s drive.  It’s cheaper.
Don’t go by train.  It’s more expensive.
Cheaper an

units.title: Comparison 2 (much better etc. / any better? etc.)
units.search_text:
any/no ➜ Unit 86
Comparative 1, 3 ➜ Units 105, 107
eldest ➜ Unit 108C 
even + comparative ➜ Unit 112C
A
Comparative 2 (much better / any better etc.)
much / a lot etc. + comparative
Before comparatives you can use:
much 
 
a lot 
 
far (= a lot) 
 
a bit 
 
a little 
 
slightly (= a little)
I felt i

units.title: Comparison 3 (as ... as / than)
units.search_text:
Comparative 1–2 ➜ Units 105–106
as long as ➜ Unit 115B
as and like ➜ Unit 117
A
Comparative 3 (as … as / than)
Study this example situation:
Some more examples of not as … (as):
Jack isn’t as old as he looks.  (= he looks older than he is)
The town centre wasn’t as crowded as usual.  (= it is usuall

units.title: Superlatives (the longest, the most enjoyable etc.)
units.search_text:
A
Superlative (the longest / the most enjoyable etc.)
Look at these examples:
The superlative form is -est or most …  .  In general, we use -est for short words and most … for
longer words. 
 long → longest  
hot → hottest 
easy → easiest 
hard → hardest
but    most famous 
most boring 
most enjoyab

units.title: Word order 1: verb + object; place and time
units.search_text:
A
Word order 1: verb + object; place and time
Verb + object
The verb and the object normally go together.  We do not usually put other words between them:
I
Our guide 
I didn’t
Do you
verb
like
spoke
use
eat
object
my job 
English 
my phone
meat
very much. (not I like very much)
fluently.    (not sp

units.title: Word order 2: adverbs with the verb
units.search_text:
Word order 1 ➜ Unit 109
A
Word order 2: adverbs with the verb
Some adverbs (for example, always, also, probably) go with the verb in the middle of a sentence:
Emily always drives to work.
We were feeling very tired and we were also hungry.
The meeting will probably be cancelled.
Unit
110
If the verb

units.title: Still, yet and already / Any more, any longer, no longer
units.search_text:
A
still 
any more 
yet 
already
We use still to say that a situation or action is continuing.  It hasn’t changed or stopped:
It’s ten o’clock and Joe is still in bed.
When I went to bed, Chris was still working.
Do you still want to go away or have you changed your mind?
Still also means ‘in spite o

units.title: Even
units.search_text:
if and when ➜ Unit 25D
though / even though ➜ Unit 113E
A
Study this example situation:
Some more examples:
These pictures are really awful.  Even I take better pictures than these.
(and I’m certainly not a good photographer)
He always wears a coat, even in hot weather.
The print was very small.  I

units.title: Although / though / even though / In spite of / despite
units.search_text:
A
even ➜ Unit 112
Study this example situation:
although 
though 
even though
in spite of 
despite
Unit
113
Last year Paul and Sarah had a holiday by the sea.
It rained a lot, but they had a good time.
You can say:
Although it rained a lot, they had a good time.
(= It rained a lot, but they …)
or
In

units.title: In case
units.search_text:
Study this example situation:
Some more examples of in case:
I’d better write down my password in case I forget it. 
(= because it is possible I will forget it)
Shall I draw a map for you in case you have a problem finding our house? 
(= because it is possible you will have problems finding it)
I’ll

units.title: Unless / As long as / Provided
units.search_text:
A
unless
Study this example situation:
unless 
 
as long as 
 
provided
Unit
115
The club is for members only.
You can’t go in unless you are a member.
This means:
You can’t go in except if you are a member.
You can go in only if you are a member.
unless = except if 
Some more examples of unless:
I’

units.title: As (As I walked ... / As I didn't ... etc.)
units.search_text:
A
as … as ➜ Unit 107
like and as ➜ Unit 117
as if ➜ Unit 118
as = at the same time as
You can use as when two things happen together at the same time:
We all waved goodbye to Liz as she drove away.
 
(we waved and she drove away at the same time)
As I walked along the street, I looked in the 
shop w

units.title: Like and as
units.search_text:
as … as ➜ Unit 107
as (= at the same time as / because) ➜ Unit 116
as if ➜ Unit 118
A
like and as
Like = similar to, the same as:
What a beautiful house!  It’s like a palace. (not as a palace)
Be careful!  The floor has been polished.  It’s like walking on ice. (not as walking)
It’s raining again.

units.title: Like / as if / as though
units.search_text:
if I was/were ➜ Unit 39C
look/sound etc. + adjective ➜ Unit 99C
like and as ➜ Unit 117
A
We use like or as if to say how somebody/something looks, sounds or feels:
That house looks like it’s going to fall down.    or
That house looks as if it’s going to fall down.
Amy sounded like she had a cold, di

units.title: For, during and while
units.search_text:
for and since ➜ Unit 12A
while + -ing ➜ Unit 68B
during
during = at a time between the start and end of something:
I fell asleep during the movie. (= at a time between
the start and end of the movie)
We met some really nice people during our holiday.
The ground is wet.  It must have rained during th

units.title: By and until / By the time ...
units.search_text:
A
will be doing and will have done ➜ Unit 24
by (other uses) ➜ Units 42B, 60B, 128
by … = not later than:
by and until 
by the time …
Unit
120
I sent the documents today, so they should arrive 
by Monday.
(= on or before Monday, not later than Monday)
We’d better hurry.  We have to be home by 5 o’cl

units.title: At/on/in (time)
units.search_text:
A
at/on/in (time)
Compare at, on and in:
They arrived at 5 o’clock.
They arrived on Friday.
They arrived in June. / They arrived in 2012.
We use:
Unit
121
at for the time of day
at five o’clock 
 
at 11.45 
 
at midnight 
 
at lunchtime 
 
at sunset 
 
etc.
on for days and dates
on Friday / on Frida

units.title: On time and in time / At the end and in the end
units.search_text:
at/on/in (time) ➜ Unit 121
A
on time and in time      at the end and in the end
on time and in time
Unit
122
on time = punctual, not late
If something happens on time, it happens at the time that was planned:
The 11.45 train left on time. (= it left at 11.45)
Please be on time.  Don’t be late. 
The

units.title: In/at/on (position) 1
units.search_text:
in/at/on (position) 2–3 ➜ Units 124–125
A
in/at/on  (position) 1
in
Unit
123
There’s no-one in the room / in the building / in the garden.
What do you have in your hand / in your mouth?
When we were in Italy, we spent a few days in Venice.
I have a friend who lives in a small village in the mountain

units.title: In/at/on (position) 2
units.search_text:
A
in/at/on  (position) 2
We say that somebody/something is: 
in a line, in a row, in a queue
in a picture, in a photo(graph)
in a newspaper, in a magazine, in a book
in an off ice, in a department
in the sky, in the world
in th e country (= not in a town)
When I go to the cinema, I like to sit in th

units.title: In/at/on (position) 3
units.search_text:
A
in/at/on   (position) 3
in hospital / at work etc.
We say that somebody is in bed / in hospital / in prison:
James isn’t up yet.  He’s still in bed.
Anna’s mother is in hospital.
We say that somebody is at home / at work / at school / at university / at college:
I’ll be at work until 5.30.
My sist

units.title: To, at, in and into
units.search_text:
A
to, at, in and into
We say go/come/travel (etc.) to a place or event.  For example:
go to China
go back to Italy
return to London
go to work
go to the bank
go to a party
come to my house
drive to the airport
be taken to hospital
When are your friends going back to Italy? (not going back in Italy)

units.title: In/at/on (other uses)
units.search_text:
in/at/on (time) ➜ Unit 121
in/at/on (position) ➜ Units 123–125
A
in/on/at  (other uses)
in
in the rain / in the sun / in the shade / in the dark / in bad weather etc.
We sat in the shade.  It was too hot to sit in the sun.
Don’t go out in the rain.  Wait until it stops.
in a language / in a currency

units.title: By
units.search_text:
A
by
You can say that something happens by mistake / by accident / by chance:
We hadn’t arranged to meet.  We met by chance.
But we say ‘do something on purpose’ (= you mean to do it):
I didn’t do it on purpose.  It was an accident.
Note that we say by chance, by accident etc. (not by the chance / b

units.title: Noun + preposition (reason for, cause of etc.)
units.search_text:
A
Noun + preposition  (reason for, cause of etc.)
noun + for …
Unit
129
a demand / a need FOR …
The company went out of business.  There was no demand for its product any more.
There’s no excuse for behaviour like that.  There’s no need for it.
a picture / a photo / a photograph / a map / a plan / a

units.title: Adjective + preposition 1
units.search_text:
A
Adjective + preposition 1
nice of you, nice to me
Unit
130
nice / kind / good / generous / polite / honest / stupid / silly etc. OF somebody (to do something)
Thank you.  It was very nice of you to help me.
It was stupid of me to go out without a coat in such cold weather.
(be) nice / kind / good

units.title: Adjective + preposition 2
units.search_text:
A
Adjective + preposition 2
adjective + of 
Unit
131
afraid / scared / frightened / terrified OF …
‘Are you afraid of spiders?’ 
‘Yes, I’m terrified of them.’
good / bad / brilliant / better / hopeless etc. AT …
I’m not very good at repairing things. (not good in repairing things)
fond / proud / ash

units.title: Verb + preposition 1 to and at
units.search_text:
A
Verb + preposition 1   to and at
verb + to
Unit
132
talk / speak TO somebody (talk/speak with is also possible)
Who were you talking to?
listen TO …
When I’m driving, I like to listen to the radio. (not listen the radio)
ask somebody (a question)
If there’s anything you want to know, you can ask m

units.title: Verb + preposition 2 about/for/of/after
units.search_text:
Verbs + about/of (think/hear etc.) ➜ Unit 134
Other verbs + for ➜ Unit 135B
A
Verb + preposition 2   about/for/of/after
verb + about
Unit
133
talk / read / know ABOUT … 
We talked about a lot of things at the meeting.
do something/nothing ABOUT something = do something/nothing to improve a situation

units.title: Verb + preposition 3 about and of
units.search_text:
A
Verb + preposition 3   about and of
Unit
134
hear ABOUT … = be told about something
Did you hear about the fire at the hotel?
Sometimes the difference is very small and you can use of or about:
When I’m alone, I often think of you.    or    … think about you.
You can say think of or think about do

units.title: Verb + preposition 4 of/for/from/on
units.search_text:
Verb + preposition + -ing ➜ Unit 62
Other verbs + for ➜ Unit 133
Other verbs + on ➜ Unit 136E
A
Verb + preposition 4   of/for/from/on
verb + of
Unit
135
accuse / suspect somebody OF …
Tina accused me of being selfish.
Some students were suspected of cheating in the exam.
congratulate / compliment so

units.title: Verb + preposition 5 in/into/with/to/on
units.search_text:
A
Verb + preposition 5    in/into/with/to/on
verb + in
Unit
136
believe IN … = believe that something exists, believe that it’s good to do something
Do you believe in God? (= do you believe that God exists?)
 I believe in saying what I think. (= I believe it is right to say what I think)
but ‘believ

units.title: Phrasal verbs 1 Introduction
units.search_text:
Phrasal verbs 2–9 ➜ Units 138–145
American English ➜ Appendix 7
A
Phrasal verbs 1     Introduction
We often use verbs with:
in
out
on
off
up
down
away
back
by
through
about
along
over
forward
round or around
So you can say look out / get on / take off / run away etc.  These are phrasal verbs.
We oft

units.title: Phrasal verbs 2 in/out
units.search_text:
A
Phrasal verbs 2   in/out
Compare in and out:
Unit
138
in = into a room, a building, a car etc.
How did the thieves get in?
Here’s a key, so you can let yourself in.
Lisa walked up to the edge of the pool
 
and dived in. (= into the water)
I’ve got a new apartment.  I’m moving in
on Friday.
As soon

units.title: Phrasal verbs 3 out
units.search_text:
A
Phrasal verbs 3 
out
Unit
139
out = not burning, not shining
go out
put out a fire / a cigarette / a light
turn out a light
blow out a candle
Suddenly all the lights in the building went out.
I put the fire out with a fire extinguisher.
I turned the lights out before leaving.
We don’t need the can

units.title: Phrasal verbs 4 on/off (1)
units.search_text:
Phrasal verbs 1 (Introduction) ➜ Unit 137
More verbs + on/off ➜ Unit 141
A
Phrasal verbs 4     on/off (1)
on and off for lights, machines etc.
Unit
140
We say:
the light is on / put the light on / leave the light on etc.
turn the light on/off    or    switch the light on/off
Shall I leave the lights

units.title: Phrasal verbs 5 on/off (2)
units.search_text:
A
Phrasal verbs 5 on/oﬀ (2)
verb + on = continue doing something
Unit
141
go on = continue
The party went on until 4 o’clock in the morning.
keep on doing (or keep doing) something = do it continuously or repeatedly
 He keeps on criticising me.  It’s not fair! (or He keeps criticising me.)
drive on

units.title: Phrasal verbs 6 up (1)
units.search_text:
Phrasal verbs 1 (Introduction) ➜ Unit 137
More verbs + up ➜ Units 143–144
A
Phrasal verbs 6 up/down
Compare up and down:
Unit
142
put something up (on a wall etc.)
I put a picture up on
the wall.
pick something up
There was a letter on the floor.
 
I picked it up and looked at it.
stand up
Alan stoo

units.title: Phrasal verbs 7 up (2)
units.search_text:
Phrasal verbs 1 (Introduction) ➜ Unit 137
More verbs + up ➜ Units 142, 144
A
Phrasal verbs 7 
up (1)
Unit
143
go up, come up, walk up (to …) = approach
A man came up to me in the street and asked me for money.
set up an organisation, a company, a business, a system, a website etc. = start it
The gov

units.title: Phrasal verbs 8 away/back
units.search_text:
A
Phrasal verbs 8     up (2)
Unit
144
bring up a topic etc. = introduce it in a conversation
I don’t want to hear any more about this.  Please don’t bring it up again.
make something up = invent something that is not true
What Kevin told you about himself wasn’t true.  He made it all up.
save up for

units.title: Phrasal verbs 9 about/round/forward/over
units.search_text:
Phrasal verbs 1 (Introduction) ➜ Unit 137
A
Phrasal verbs 9    away/back
Unit
145
Compare away and back:
away = away from home
We’re going away on holiday today.
away = away from a place, a person etc.
Sarah got into her car, started the engine  
and drove away.
I tried to take a picture of the bird
